questTitle: O-Level Chemistry: Metallic Bonding and Alloys Worksheet 2
subject: Chemistry
description: Master metallic bonding and alloys! This worksheet covers the structure of metals, metallic bonding, properties of metals, formation and properties of alloys, and how structure relates to properties. Understand delocalised electrons, electrical conductivity, malleability, and why alloys are harder than pure metals!

questions.problemSet: CONCEPT CHECK: Metallic Bonding
questions.question: What is metallic bonding?
options: The sharing of electrons between metal atoms
The transfer of electrons from one metal atom to another
The electrostatic attraction between positive metal ions and a 'sea' of delocalised electrons
The weak forces between metal molecules
questions.answer: The electrostatic attraction between positive metal ions and a 'sea' of delocalised electrons

questions.problemSet: CONCEPT CHECK: Metallic Bonding
questions.question: In metallic bonding, what happens to the valence electrons of metal atoms?
options: They are transferred to non-metal atoms
They are shared between two specific metal atoms
They become delocalised and move freely throughout the metal structure
They remain fixed around each metal atom
questions.answer: They become delocalised and move freely throughout the metal structure

questions.problemSet: CONCEPT CHECK: Metallic Structure
questions.question: Describe the arrangement of particles in a metallic structure.
options: Metal molecules arranged randomly
Positive metal ions arranged in a regular lattice, surrounded by a 'sea' of delocalised electrons
Alternating positive and negative ions in a lattice
Atoms sharing electrons in a tetrahedral arrangement
questions.answer: Positive metal ions arranged in a regular lattice, surrounded by a 'sea' of delocalised electrons

questions.problemSet: CONCEPT CHECK: Metallic Structure
questions.question: What type of particles are present in a metal structure?
options: Only neutral metal atoms
Positive metal ions and delocalised electrons
Positive and negative ions
Metal molecules
questions.answer: Positive metal ions and delocalised electrons

questions.problemSet: TOPIC 1: Structure and Bonding in Metals
questions.question: When sodium atoms form a metallic structure, what happens to the valence electron of each sodium atom?
options: It is transferred to another sodium atom
It becomes delocalised and joins the 'sea' of electrons, leaving behind Na⁺ ions
It is shared with one other sodium atom
It remains in its original position
questions.answer: It becomes delocalised and joins the 'sea' of electrons, leaving behind Na⁺ ions

questions.problemSet: TOPIC 1: Structure and Bonding in Metals
questions.question: Magnesium has 2 valence electrons. In metallic magnesium, what ions are formed and how many delocalised electrons does each magnesium atom contribute?
options: Mg⁺ ions; 1 delocalised electron per atom
Mg²⁺ ions; 2 delocalised electrons per atom
Mg²⁻ ions; 2 electrons gained per atom
No ions are formed
questions.answer: Mg²⁺ ions; 2 delocalised electrons per atom

questions.problemSet: TOPIC 1: Structure and Bonding in Metals
questions.question: What holds the metal structure together?
options: Covalent bonds between metal atoms
Weak intermolecular forces
Strong electrostatic forces of attraction between the positive metal ions and the delocalised electrons
Ionic bonds between metal ions
questions.answer: Strong electrostatic forces of attraction between the positive metal ions and the delocalised electrons

questions.problemSet: TOPIC 1: Structure and Bonding in Metals ⭐
questions.question: Compare the metallic bonding in sodium (1 valence electron) and magnesium (2 valence electrons). Which would you expect to have stronger metallic bonding?
options: Sodium, because it has fewer electrons
Magnesium, because Mg²⁺ has a higher charge than Na⁺ and there are more delocalised electrons, resulting in stronger electrostatic attraction
Both have the same strength of bonding
Sodium, because it is more reactive
questions.answer: Magnesium, because Mg²⁺ has a higher charge than Na⁺ and there are more delocalised electrons, resulting in stronger electrostatic attraction

questions.problemSet: TOPIC 2: Properties of Metals - Electrical Conductivity
questions.question: Why do metals conduct electricity?
options: Because metal ions can move through the structure
Because delocalised electrons are free to move throughout the structure and carry charge
Because metals contain negative ions
Because metals have weak bonds
questions.answer: Because delocalised electrons are free to move throughout the structure and carry charge

questions.problemSet: TOPIC 2: Properties of Metals - Electrical Conductivity
questions.question: When a metal conducts electricity, what particles actually move to carry the current?
options: Positive metal ions
Negative metal ions
Delocalised electrons
Protons
questions.answer: Delocalised electrons

questions.problemSet: TOPIC 2: Properties of Metals - Electrical Conductivity
questions.question: Copper is used in electrical wiring. Why is copper suitable for this purpose?
options: Copper is magnetic
Copper has delocalised electrons that can move freely and carry charge, making it a good electrical conductor
Copper is very hard
Copper has a low melting point
questions.answer: Copper has delocalised electrons that can move freely and carry charge, making it a good electrical conductor

questions.problemSet: TOPIC 2: Properties of Metals - Thermal Conductivity
questions.question: Why are metals good conductors of heat?
options: Because they have high melting points
Because delocalised electrons can move and transfer kinetic energy throughout the structure
Because metal ions vibrate and transfer heat
Because metals have weak bonds
questions.answer: Because delocalised electrons can move and transfer kinetic energy throughout the structure

questions.problemSet: TOPIC 2: Properties of Metals - Malleability and Ductility
questions.question: Metals are malleable (can be hammered into sheets) and ductile (can be drawn into wires). Why?
options: Because metallic bonds are weak and break easily
Because when force is applied, layers of metal ions can slide over each other while the delocalised electrons continue to hold the structure together
Because metals contain molecules that can move
Because metals have covalent bonds that stretch
questions.answer: Because when force is applied, layers of metal ions can slide over each other while the delocalised electrons continue to hold the structure together

questions.problemSet: TOPIC 2: Properties of Metals - Malleability and Ductility
questions.question: When a metal is bent, what happens to the metallic bonding?
options: All metallic bonds break permanently
Layers of ions slide over each other, but the delocalised electrons adjust and maintain the metallic bonding
The metal becomes ionic
The electrons escape from the metal
questions.answer: Layers of ions slide over each other, but the delocalised electrons adjust and maintain the metallic bonding

questions.problemSet: TOPIC 2: Properties of Metals - Melting Point
questions.question: Most metals have high melting points. Why?
options: Because metals have weak metallic bonds
Because a large amount of energy is needed to overcome the strong electrostatic forces of attraction between positive metal ions and delocalised electrons
Because metals have covalent bonds
Because metals are very dense
questions.answer: Because a large amount of energy is needed to overcome the strong electrostatic forces of attraction between positive metal ions and delocalised electrons

questions.problemSet: TOPIC 2: Properties of Metals ⭐
questions.question: Explain why magnesium has a higher melting point than sodium.
options: Magnesium atoms are larger than sodium atoms
Magnesium has Mg²⁺ ions with a higher charge and more delocalised electrons than sodium (Na⁺), resulting in stronger electrostatic forces of attraction
Magnesium has weaker metallic bonding
Sodium has more delocalised electrons
questions.answer: Magnesium has Mg²⁺ ions with a higher charge and more delocalised electrons than sodium (Na⁺), resulting in stronger electrostatic forces of attraction

questions.problemSet: TOPIC 3: Introduction to Alloys
questions.question: What is an alloy?
options: A pure metal
A compound of two metals
A mixture of a metal with one or more other elements (usually metals or carbon)
A metal dissolved in water
questions.answer: A mixture of a metal with one or more other elements (usually metals or carbon)

questions.problemSet: TOPIC 3: Introduction to Alloys
questions.question: Which of the following is an example of an alloy?
options: Pure iron
Pure copper
Steel (iron mixed with carbon)
Sodium chloride
questions.answer: Steel (iron mixed with carbon)

questions.problemSet: TOPIC 3: Introduction to Alloys
questions.question: Bronze is an alloy of copper and tin. Brass is an alloy of copper and zinc. What type of substances are bronze and brass?
options: Pure metals
Compounds
Alloys (mixtures of metals)
Ionic compounds
questions.answer: Alloys (mixtures of metals)

questions.problemSet: TOPIC 3: Introduction to Alloys
questions.question: Steel is made by adding a small amount of carbon to iron. Why is steel considered an alloy?
options: Because steel is a compound of iron and carbon
Because steel is a mixture of iron with carbon, where carbon atoms are mixed into the iron structure
Because steel is pure iron
Because carbon removes impurities from iron
questions.answer: Because steel is a mixture of iron with carbon, where carbon atoms are mixed into the iron structure

questions.problemSet: TOPIC 4: Structure of Alloys
questions.question: In a pure metal, all the metal ions are the same size. What happens in an alloy?
options: All ions remain the same size
Atoms/ions of different sizes are present in the structure
The metal becomes a compound
All atoms become neutral
questions.answer: Atoms/ions of different sizes are present in the structure

questions.problemSet: TOPIC 4: Structure of Alloys
questions.question: In a pure metal, the layers of ions are arranged in a regular pattern. How does adding atoms of a different size (as in an alloy) affect this arrangement?
options: The arrangement becomes more regular
The different-sized atoms disrupt the regular arrangement of layers
No change occurs
The metal becomes liquid
questions.answer: The different-sized atoms disrupt the regular arrangement of layers

questions.problemSet: TOPIC 4: Structure of Alloys
questions.question: Why are alloys generally harder than pure metals?
options: Alloys have weaker metallic bonding
The different-sized atoms in alloys disrupt the regular arrangement of layers, making it harder for layers to slide over each other
Alloys contain ionic bonds
Alloys have fewer delocalised electrons
questions.answer: The different-sized atoms in alloys disrupt the regular arrangement of layers, making it harder for layers to slide over each other

questions.problemSet: TOPIC 4: Structure of Alloys ⭐
questions.question: In pure iron, layers of Fe²⁺/Fe³⁺ ions can slide over each other easily, making iron relatively soft. When carbon atoms are added to make steel, the steel is harder. Explain why.
options: Carbon atoms make the metallic bonding weaker
Carbon atoms are different in size from iron atoms/ions, disrupting the regular arrangement and preventing layers from sliding over each other easily
Carbon atoms remove electrons from the iron
Carbon atoms form covalent bonds with iron
questions.answer: Carbon atoms are different in size from iron atoms/ions, disrupting the regular arrangement and preventing layers from sliding over each other easily

questions.problemSet: TOPIC 5: Properties of Alloys vs Pure Metals
questions.question: Compare the hardness of pure copper and bronze (copper + tin).
options: Pure copper is harder than bronze
Bronze is harder than pure copper because tin atoms disrupt the regular arrangement of copper ions
Both have the same hardness
Bronze is softer because it contains two metals
questions.answer: Bronze is harder than pure copper because tin atoms disrupt the regular arrangement of copper ions

questions.problemSet: TOPIC 5: Properties of Alloys vs Pure Metals
questions.question: Pure gold (24 karat) is soft and easily scratched. Jewelry is often made from 18 karat gold (gold mixed with other metals like silver and copper). Why?
options: 18 karat gold is cheaper
18 karat gold is an alloy and is harder than pure gold because the added atoms disrupt the regular arrangement, making it more durable for jewelry
18 karat gold is softer and easier to shape
Pure gold is too hard for jewelry
questions.answer: 18 karat gold is an alloy and is harder than pure gold because the added atoms disrupt the regular arrangement, making it more durable for jewelry

questions.problemSet: TOPIC 5: Properties of Alloys vs Pure Metals
questions.question: Do alloys conduct electricity?
options: No, because the structure is disrupted
Yes, because alloys still contain delocalised electrons that can move and carry charge
Only some alloys conduct electricity
No, because alloys are not metals
questions.answer: Yes, because alloys still contain delocalised electrons that can move and carry charge

questions.problemSet: TOPIC 5: Properties of Alloys vs Pure Metals
questions.question: Stainless steel (iron + chromium + nickel) is more resistant to rusting than pure iron. This is an example of how alloying can:
options: Decrease the melting point
Improve specific properties like corrosion resistance
Make the metal softer
Reduce electrical conductivity to zero
questions.answer: Improve specific properties like corrosion resistance

questions.problemSet: TOPIC 5: Properties of Alloys vs Pure Metals ⭐
questions.question: Explain why pure aluminium is not used for aircraft construction, but aluminium alloys (e.g., duralumin - aluminium + copper + magnesium) are preferred.
options: Pure aluminium is too expensive
Pure aluminium is relatively soft and weak; aluminium alloys are harder and stronger because different-sized atoms prevent layers from sliding easily, while remaining lightweight
Aluminium alloys are lighter than pure aluminium
Pure aluminium cannot conduct electricity
questions.answer: Pure aluminium is relatively soft and weak; aluminium alloys are harder and stronger because different-sized atoms prevent layers from sliding easily, while remaining lightweight

questions.problemSet: TOPIC 6: Comparing Metallic Bonding with Other Bonding
questions.question: Compare how electrical conductivity occurs in metals and in ionic compounds.
options: Both conduct via movement of ions
Both conduct via movement of electrons
Metals conduct via delocalised electrons; ionic compounds conduct (when molten/dissolved) via mobile ions
Neither can conduct electricity
questions.answer: Metals conduct via delocalised electrons; ionic compounds conduct (when molten/dissolved) via mobile ions

questions.problemSet: TOPIC 6: Comparing Metallic Bonding with Other Bonding
questions.question: Why can metals conduct electricity in the solid state, but ionic compounds cannot?
options: Metals have stronger bonds
In metals, delocalised electrons are free to move in the solid state; in ionic compounds, ions are fixed in position in the solid lattice
Ionic compounds have electrons that cannot move
Metals contain ions while ionic compounds do not
questions.answer: In metals, delocalised electrons are free to move in the solid state; in ionic compounds, ions are fixed in position in the solid lattice

questions.problemSet: TOPIC 6: Comparing Metallic Bonding with Other Bonding
questions.question: Compare why metals are malleable but ionic compounds are brittle.
options: Metals have weaker bonds than ionic compounds
In metals, layers can slide and electrons adjust to maintain bonding; in ionic compounds, if layers shift, ions of the same charge align and repel, causing the crystal to shatter
Ionic compounds have stronger bonds
Metals and ionic compounds are both malleable
questions.answer: In metals, layers can slide and electrons adjust to maintain bonding; in ionic compounds, if layers shift, ions of the same charge align and repel, causing the crystal to shatter

questions.problemSet: TOPIC 6: Comparing Metallic Bonding with Other Bonding
questions.question: Both graphite (giant covalent) and metals can conduct electricity. Compare how they conduct.
options: Both conduct via mobile ions
Both conduct via delocalised electrons that are free to move
Graphite conducts via ions; metals conduct via electrons
Graphite conducts via covalent bonds; metals conduct via ionic bonds
questions.answer: Both conduct via delocalised electrons that are free to move

questions.problemSet: TOPIC 6: Comparing Metallic Bonding with Other Bonding ⭐
questions.question: Compare the bonding, structure, and properties of diamond (giant covalent) and iron (metallic).
options: Both have delocalised electrons and conduct electricity
Diamond: strong covalent bonds, no free electrons, hard, does not conduct. Iron: metallic bonding (metal ions + delocalised electrons), malleable, conducts electricity
Both are hard and do not conduct electricity
Both have layered structures
questions.answer: Diamond: strong covalent bonds, no free electrons, hard, does not conduct. Iron: metallic bonding (metal ions + delocalised electrons), malleable, conducts electricity

questions.problemSet: TOPIC 7: Applications of Metals and Alloys
questions.question: Copper is used for electrical wiring and water pipes. Which properties make it suitable?
options: Low melting point and brittleness
Good electrical conductivity (delocalised electrons), ductility (can be drawn into wires), and resistance to corrosion
Hardness and high reactivity
Low density and poor conductivity
questions.answer: Good electrical conductivity (delocalised electrons), ductility (can be drawn into wires), and resistance to corrosion

questions.problemSet: TOPIC 7: Applications of Metals and Alloys
questions.question: Aluminium is used for aircraft bodies and food packaging. Which properties make it suitable?
options: High density and reactivity
Low density (lightweight), resistance to corrosion (protective oxide layer), and malleability
High melting point and hardness
Good electrical conductivity only
questions.answer: Low density (lightweight), resistance to corrosion (protective oxide layer), and malleability

questions.problemSet: TOPIC 7: Applications of Metals and Alloys
questions.question: Steel is used in construction (buildings, bridges). Why is steel preferred over pure iron?
options: Steel is softer than pure iron
Steel is harder and stronger than pure iron because carbon atoms disrupt the regular arrangement of iron atoms/ions
Steel is lighter than pure iron
Steel does not conduct electricity
questions.answer: Steel is harder and stronger than pure iron because carbon atoms disrupt the regular arrangement of iron atoms/ions

questions.problemSet: TOPIC 7: Applications of Metals and Alloys
questions.question: Solder (traditionally tin + lead) is used to join electrical components. Why is an alloy used rather than a pure metal?
options: Alloys do not conduct electricity
Alloys can be designed to have specific properties, such as a lower melting point than the pure metals, making them easier to work with
Pure metals are too expensive
Alloys are always harder than pure metals
questions.answer: Alloys can be designed to have specific properties, such as a lower melting point than the pure metals, making them easier to work with

questions.problemSet: TOPIC 7: Applications of Metals and Alloys ⭐
questions.question: Titanium alloys are used in hip replacements and aircraft engines. Suggest why titanium alloys are chosen for these applications.
options: Titanium alloys are cheap and soft
Titanium alloys are strong, lightweight, resistant to corrosion, and biocompatible (for medical implants)
Titanium alloys are the heaviest alloys available
Titanium alloys have low melting points
questions.answer: Titanium alloys are strong, lightweight, resistant to corrosion, and biocompatible (for medical implants)

questions.problemSet: TOPIC 8: Error Analysis
questions.question: A student states: 'Metals conduct electricity because metal atoms can move through the structure.' Is this correct?
options: Yes, metal atoms carry the current
No. Metal ions are fixed in position. Metals conduct because delocalised electrons are free to move through the structure and carry charge
Yes, both atoms and electrons move
No, metals cannot conduct electricity
questions.answer: No. Metal ions are fixed in position. Metals conduct because delocalised electrons are free to move through the structure and carry charge

questions.problemSet: TOPIC 8: Error Analysis
questions.question: A student states: 'Alloys are harder than pure metals because alloys have stronger metallic bonding.' Is this correct?
options: Yes, alloying increases the strength of metallic bonds
No. Alloys are harder because different-sized atoms disrupt the regular arrangement of layers, preventing layers from sliding easily. The strength of metallic bonding is not necessarily increased
Yes, more electrons means stronger bonding
No, alloys are softer than pure metals
questions.answer: No. Alloys are harder because different-sized atoms disrupt the regular arrangement of layers, preventing layers from sliding easily. The strength of metallic bonding is not necessarily increased

questions.problemSet: TOPIC 8: Error Analysis
questions.question: A student states: 'Steel is a compound of iron and carbon.' Is this correct?
options: Yes, steel has a fixed formula
No. Steel is an alloy (mixture) of iron with a small amount of carbon. The composition can vary, and no chemical bonds form between iron and carbon
Yes, iron and carbon react chemically
No, steel contains no carbon
questions.answer: No. Steel is an alloy (mixture) of iron with a small amount of carbon. The composition can vary, and no chemical bonds form between iron and carbon

questions.problemSet: TOPIC 8: Error Analysis ⭐
questions.question: A student states: 'Metals are malleable because metallic bonds are weak.' Identify and correct the error.
options: The statement is correct
Error: Metallic bonds are strong, not weak. Metals are malleable because layers of ions can slide over each other while delocalised electrons adjust to maintain the bonding
Error: Metals are not malleable
Error: Metallic bonds do not exist
questions.answer: Error: Metallic bonds are strong, not weak. Metals are malleable because layers of ions can slide over each other while delocalised electrons adjust to maintain the bonding

questions.problemSet: TOPIC 9: Summary Comparisons
questions.question:
Complete the comparison:

| Property | Pure Metal | Alloy |
|----------|------------|-------|
| Arrangement of atoms | Regular | ? |
| Hardness | ? | Usually harder |
options: Alloy: Regular; Pure metal hardness: Harder than alloy
Alloy: Disrupted/irregular (different-sized atoms); Pure metal hardness: Softer (layers slide easily)
Alloy: Regular; Pure metal hardness: Same as alloy
Alloy: Layers present; Pure metal hardness: Very hard
questions.answer: Alloy: Disrupted/irregular (different-sized atoms); Pure metal hardness: Softer (layers slide easily)

questions.problemSet: TOPIC 9: Summary Comparisons
questions.question:
Compare the electrical conductivity of:

A: Copper (metal)
B: Diamond (giant covalent)
C: Sodium chloride solution (ionic)
options: All three conduct via the same mechanism
A: Conducts (delocalised electrons); B: Does not conduct (no free electrons); C: Conducts (mobile ions)
None of them conduct electricity
A: Does not conduct; B: Conducts; C: Does not conduct
questions.answer: A: Conducts (delocalised electrons); B: Does not conduct (no free electrons); C: Conducts (mobile ions)

questions.problemSet: COMPREHENSIVE CHALLENGE
questions.question: Explain, in terms of structure and bonding, why copper is used for electrical wiring while steel is used for construction.
options: Both have the same properties but different costs

Copper: excellent electrical conductor (delocalised electrons), ductile (can be drawn into wires). Steel: hard and strong (carbon atoms disrupt iron structure, preventing layers from sliding), suitable for load-bearing structures
Steel conducts electricity better than copper
Copper is harder than steel
questions.answer: Copper: excellent electrical conductor (delocalised electrons), ductile (can be drawn into wires). Steel: hard and strong (carbon atoms disrupt iron structure, preventing layers from sliding), suitable for load-bearing structures

questions.problemSet: COMPREHENSIVE CHALLENGE
questions.question: A pure metal X is soft and conducts electricity. When a small amount of element Y is added, the resulting alloy is much harder but still conducts electricity. Explain these observations.
options: Element Y increases the number of delocalised electrons

In pure metal X, layers of ions slide easily (soft), and delocalised electrons allow conduction. Adding Y introduces different-sized atoms that disrupt the regular arrangement (harder), but delocalised electrons are still present (still conducts)
Element Y removes all delocalised electrons
The alloy becomes ionic
questions.answer: In pure metal X, layers of ions slide easily (soft), and delocalised electrons allow conduction. Adding Y introduces different-sized atoms that disrupt the regular arrangement (harder), but delocalised electrons are still present (still conducts)

questions.problemSet: COMPREHENSIVE CHALLENGE
questions.question:
Compare the structure and bonding in:

1. Graphite (giant covalent)
2. Copper (metallic)

Both conduct electricity. Explain the similarities and differences.
options: Both have identical structures

Both conduct via delocalised electrons. Graphite: carbon atoms in layers with delocalised electrons between layers. Copper: Cu⁺ ions in a lattice with delocalised electrons throughout. Graphite has weak forces between layers (soft); copper has metallic bonding throughout (malleable)
Graphite has metallic bonding; copper has covalent bonding
Neither actually conducts electricity
questions.answer: Both conduct via delocalised electrons. Graphite: carbon atoms in layers with delocalised electrons between layers. Copper: Cu⁺ ions in a lattice with delocalised electrons throughout. Graphite has weak forces between layers (soft); copper has metallic bonding throughout (malleable)

questions.problemSet: COMPREHENSIVE CHALLENGE
questions.question: A jeweler wants to make a ring that is durable and does not scratch easily. Should they use pure gold (24 karat) or 18 karat gold (alloy with silver and copper)? Explain.
options: Pure gold, because it is more valuable
18 karat gold, because the added silver and copper atoms disrupt the regular arrangement of gold atoms, making it harder and more scratch-resistant while maintaining the gold appearance
Pure gold, because alloys are too soft
18 karat gold, because it has stronger metallic bonds
questions.answer: 18 karat gold, because the added silver and copper atoms disrupt the regular arrangement of gold atoms, making it harder and more scratch-resistant while maintaining the gold appearance

questions.problemSet: COMPREHENSIVE CHALLENGE
questions.question:
A student is asked to classify substances based on their bonding and structure. Match each substance with its correct description:

1. Iron
2. Steel
3. Diamond
4. Graphite
options: 1: Giant covalent; 2: Alloy; 3: Metallic; 4: Ionic
1: Metallic (pure metal); 2: Metallic (alloy - iron + carbon); 3: Giant covalent (4 bonds per C, hard, doesn't conduct); 4: Giant covalent (3 bonds per C, layered, conducts)
1: Ionic; 2: Covalent; 3: Metallic; 4: Simple molecular
All are metallic structures
questions.answer: 1: Metallic (pure metal); 2: Metallic (alloy - iron + carbon); 3: Giant covalent (4 bonds per C, hard, doesn't conduct); 4: Giant covalent (3 bonds per C, layered, conducts)
